name: Alice's Adventures in Wonderland
author: Lewis Carroll
description:
A girl follows a White Rabbit down a hole into a realm of nonsense, riddles, and impossible tea parties.

This volume collects the opening chapters — enough to taste the logic games and strange court etiquette that await deeper study.

name: Treasure Island
author: Robert Louis Stevenson
description:
Young Jim Hawkins inherits a map to buried gold and sails with Squire Trelawney toward an island haunted by pirates.

The opening establishes the Admiral Benbow inn, Billy Bones, and the black spot — the prelude to Long John Silver's treacherous charm.

name: The Time Machine
author: H. G. Wells
description:
An inventor claims to have crossed time itself, returning with tales of humanity's far future.

He describes the gentle Eloi and the dreadful Morlocks — a world split between surface idyll and underground hunger.

name: The War of the Worlds
author: H. G. Wells
description:
Cylinder-shaped vessels fall from the sky near London, and Martian war machines rise from the smoke.

The narrator flees through a countryside choked with refugees while heat-rays and black smoke erase familiar roads.

name: Frankenstein; or, The Modern Prometheus
author: Mary Wollstonecraft Shelley
description:
Victor Frankenstein recounts how obsession led him to animate dead flesh on a storm-lashed night.

The creature's first awakening — a gasp, open eyes, and immediate horror from its maker — begins a tragedy of rejection and revenge.

name: Dracula
author: Bram Stoker
description:
Jonathan Harker's journal opens in a remote Transylvanian castle where his host never eats, never mirrors, and never sleeps by day.

Letters and diary entries assemble the first warnings of a predator moving toward England by sea.

name: The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes
author: Arthur Conan Doyle
description:
Dr Watson introduces the consulting detective who reads crime scenes like printed pages.

These cases begin with small oddities — a scarlet thread, a missing racehorse, a woman who vanishes on her wedding day — and end in precise explanation.

name: Around the World in Eighty Days
author: Jules Verne
description:
Phileas Fogg wagers twenty thousand pounds that he can circle the globe in eighty days — and sets out that very evening.

Passepartout, his new valet, discovers that calm routine has given way to trains, elephants, and relentless timetables.

name: Twenty Thousand Leagues under the Sea
author: Jules Verne
description:
Reports of a sea monster attacking ships lead Professor Aronnax aboard the frigate Abraham Lincoln — and then into captivity aboard a submarine.

Captain Nemo introduces a vessel that walks the ocean floor beneath waves no sun reaches.

name: A Christmas Carol
author: Charles Dickens
description:
Ebenezer Scrooge greets Christmas with contempt until four spirits interrupt his counting-house.

The first visitation — Marley's chain-laden ghost — warns that the living forge their own fetters link by link.

name: The Picture of Dorian Gray
author: Oscar Wilde
description:
Basil Hallward paints a portrait of a youth of impossible beauty while Lord Henry whispers that youth is the only thing worth having.

Dorian wishes that the picture would age instead of him — and the wish takes hold.

name: The Legend of Sleepy Hollow
author: Washington Irving
description:
Ichabod Crane, schoolmaster and superstitious suitor, competes for the hand of Katrina Van Tassel in a valley ruled by ghost stories.

On a autumn ride home he is pursued by a rider with no head — or so the countryside insists.

name: The Jungle Book
author: Rudyard Kipling
description:
Mowgli, a human cub raised by wolves, learns the Law of the Jungle from Baloo and Bagheera.

These tales open with the wolf pack's debate over whether a man-cub may live among them — and Shere Khan's vow to destroy him.

name: Ivanhoe
author: Sir Walter Scott
description:
Wilfred of Ivanhoe returns from the Crusades to a England divided between Norman lords and Saxon resentment.

Tournament lists, forest outlaws, and the siege of Torquilstone frame a romance of loyalty under a stolen crown.

name: The Last of the Mohicans
author: James Fenimore Cooper
description:
During the French and Indian War, Hawkeye guides two sisters through wilderness stalked by Huron war parties.

The opening march along Lake George sets a pace of ambush, pursuit, and fragile alliance.

name: Meditations
author: Marcus Aurelius (George Long, trans.)
description:
Marcus Aurelius writes to himself on duty, mortality, and the discipline of mind amid empire and plague.

These notes were never meant for an audience — reminders that opinion is not injury, and the present moment is all one owns.

name: The Odyssey
author: Homer (Samuel Butler, trans.)
description:
Odysseus struggles homeward ten years after Troy while suitors devour his household.

The poem opens in medias res: gods debate his fate, and Athena urges Telemachus to seek news of a father the islanders presume dead.

name: The Iliad
author: Homer (Samuel Butler, trans.)
description:
Achilles' wrath against Agamemnon sets Greek armies and Trojan walls on a path toward Hector's death.

The opening invokes the Muse and plunges into quarrel, plague, and the cost of honour on the plains of Ilion.

name: Beowulf
author: Unknown (Francis B. Gummere, trans.)
description:
The hero Beowulf sails to Denmark to face Grendel, a monster haunting Heorot hall by night.

This excerpt covers the Geats' arrival and the first boast before arm-wrestling fate in the mead-hall.

name: Le Morte d'Arthur
author: Sir Thomas Malory
description:
Malory gathers Arthurian legend into a single chronicle of swords, quests, and doomed fellowship.

The tale begins with Uther's deception and Arthur's drawing of the stone — the kingdom unified before it fractures.

name: Hamlet
author: William Shakespeare
description:
The ghost of Hamlet's father walks the ramparts of Elsinore and demands vengeance for murder most foul.

The prince's first soliloquy weighs suicide, duty, and a world that feels out of joint.

name: The Republic
author: Plato (Benjamin Jowett, trans.)
description:
Socrates asks what justice is — in a person and in a city — and imagines rulers trained from childhood to wisdom.

The allegory of the cave appears here: prisoners mistaking shadows for truth until one climbs toward daylight.

name: The Pilgrim's Progress
author: John Bunyan
description:
Christian flees the City of Destruction burdened on his back, seeking the Celestial City through Slough and Vanity Fair.

Evangelist points him toward a wicket gate — the first step on a road measured in allegory.

name: Gulliver's Travels
author: Jonathan Swift
description:
Lemuel Gulliver shipwrecks among the tiny Lilliputians, whose politics turn on the correct end at which to break an egg.

Swift's satire begins with scale and bureaucracy before voyages grow stranger still.

name: Moby-Dick; or, The Whale
author: Herman Melville
description:
Ishmael signs aboard the Pequod and meets the harpooner Queequeg before Captain Ahab reveals his hunt for the white whale.

The opening chapters meditate on the sea's pull and the camaraderie of men who have chosen water over land.

name: Pride and Prejudice
author: Jane Austen
description:
Mrs Bennet announces that Netherfield Park is let at last, and a wealthy bachelor may rescue five daughters from modest prospects.

Elizabeth Bennet's wit first collides with Mr Darcy's pride at a country assembly.

name: Jane Eyre
author: Charlotte Brontë
description:
An orphan girl survives a cruel charity school and arrives at Thornfield as governess to a guarded child.

Footsteps in the upper rooms and laughter like a goblin hint at secrets Mr Rochester will not explain.

name: The Count of Monte Cristo
author: Alexandre Dumas (père)
description:
Edmond Dantès is betrayed on the eve of his wedding and imprisoned in the Château d'If without trial.

Years later an escape and a hidden fortune transform the innocent sailor into a patient instrument of reckoning.

name: The Prince
author: Niccolò Machiavelli (W. K. Marriott, trans.)
description:
Machiavelli advises rulers on acquiring and holding power when ideal virtue and real politics diverge.

These chapters compare hereditary and new principalities — how fortune and force shape states that look solid from afar.

name: The Wonderful Wizard of Oz
author: L. Frank Baum
description:
A cyclone lifts Dorothy's house from Kansas and drops it on the Witch of the East in the land of Oz.

The Munchkins gift her the dead witch's silver shoes and point her toward the Emerald City and the wizard who might send her home.

name: The Call of the Wild
author: Jack London
description:
Buck, a comfortable estate dog in California, is stolen and sold into the Klondike sled trade.

The first blows teach him the law of club and fang — and stir an older memory of wolves running under northern stars.

name: The Art of War
author: Sun Tzu (Lionel Giles, trans.)
description:
Sun Tzu treats warfare as a matter of calculation, deception, and knowing when not to fight.

The opening chapters weigh the five constant factors — moral law, heaven, earth, the commander, and method — that decide campaigns before blades are drawn.
